elements.sent-2411-001: We revisit the problem of synchronisability for communicating automata, \ie whether the language of send messages for an asynchronous system is the same as the language of send messages with a synchronous communication.
elements.sent-2411-002: The un/decidability of the problem depends on the specific asynchronous semantics considered as well as the topology (the communication flow) of the system.
elements.sent-2411-003: Synchronisability is known to be undecidable under the peer-to-peer semantics, while it is still an open problem for mailbox communication.
elements.sent-2411-004: The problem was shown to be decidable for ring topologies.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we show that when generalising to automata with accepting states, synchronisability is undecidable under the mailbox semantics, this result is obtained by resorting to the Post Correspondence problem.
elements.sent-2411-006: In an attempt to solve the specific problem where all states are accepting, we also show that synchronisability is decidable for tree topologies (where, as well as for rings, peer-to-peer coincides with mailbox semantics).
elements.sent-2411-007: We also discuss synchronisability for multitrees in the mailbox setting.
elements.sent-2411-008: Communicating automata~\cite{brand_communicating_1983 , \ie a network of finite state automata (the participants) where transitions can be interpreted as sending or receiving actions, are a common way to model communication protocols.}
elements.sent-2411-009: This model allows to consider systems exchanging messages in either a synchronous or asynchronous way.
elements.sent-2411-010: While in the former case communication happens simultaneously, in the latter, messages are sent to buffers where they wait until they are received by other participants.
elements.sent-2411-011: Several semantics have been proposed in the literature, \eg \cite{charron_synchronous_1996,chevrou_diversity_2016,digiusto_partial_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Nonetheless, the two most prominent ones are peer-to-peer (\Ptp) communication (where between each pair of participants there are two FIFO buffers, one per direction of communication) and \Mailbox communication (where each participant has its own FIFO buffer that stores all received messages, whatever the sender).
elements.sent-2411-013: From the expressiveness point of view, when considering asynchronous communicating automata, Turing machines can be encoded with two participants and two FIFO buffers only \cite{brand_communicating_1983 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: On the other side of the spectrum, synchronous systems are equivalent to finite state automata.
elements.sent-2411-015: In order to fill the gap between these two extremes and recover some decidability, several approaches have been considered to approximate the synchronous behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-016: Results can be classified into two main families of systems.
elements.sent-2411-017: In the first one, asynchronous behaviours are limited by bounding the size of buffers.
elements.sent-2411-018: While in the second, asynchronous behaviours are bounded by only considering systems where all the executions can be related (up to different equivalence relations) to synchronous executions.
elements.sent-2411-019: Some examples of the first family are existentially ($\exists$) and universally ($\forall$) $B$-bounded systems \cite{genest_kleene_2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: A system is universally $B$-bounded if all its executions are $B$-bounded, \ie can be made with buffers of size $B$, and existentially $B$-bounded if all its executions are causally equivalent to a $B$-bounded one.
elements.sent-2411-021: If a system is $\exists$/$\forall$-$B$-bounded, model checking problems (\ie checking whether a configuration is reachable or more generally whether a monadic second order formula is satisfied) turn out to be decidable.
elements.sent-2411-022: Unfortunately deciding whether a given system is $\exists$/$\forall$-$B$-bounded, when $B$ is unknown is undecidable for \Ptp \cite{kuske_communicating_2010 and \Mailbox \cite{bollig_unifying_2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Instead, as an example of the second family, in \cite{bouajjani_completeness_2018 , the authors define the class of $k$-synchronisable systems, which requires that any execution is causally equivalent to an execution that can be divided into slices of $k$ messages, where all the sending must be done before the receiving.}
elements.sent-2411-024: In these systems, the reachability of a configuration is decidable.
elements.sent-2411-025: Moreover, deciding whether a given \Ptp system is $k$-synchronisable is decidable \cite{bouajjani_completeness_2018 , and the same for a given \Mailbox system \cite{digiusto_k-synchronizability_2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: It is also decidable whether there exists a $k$ such that a given \Mailbox system is $k$-synchronisable \cite{giusto_guessing_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: The causal equivalence relation \cite{digiusto_k-synchronizability_2020 derives from the happened before relation \cite{lamport_time_1978 , which ensures that actions are performed in the same order, from the point of view of each participant.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: It allows to compare and group executions into classes.
elements.sent-2411-029: In \cite{basu_deciding_2016 , the authors define a different notion of synchronisability, which does not rely on causal equivalence, but on send traces (the projection of executions onto send actions).}
elements.sent-2411-030: A system is synchronisable if each asynchronous execution has the same send trace as a synchronous execution of the system.
elements.sent-2411-031: This differs from k-synchronisability, since actions may be performed in a different order by a participant, and the class of k-synchronisable systems and the one of synchronisable systems are incomparable.
elements.sent-2411-032: Synchronisability of a system implies that reachability is decidable in it.
elements.sent-2411-033: A way of checking if a system is synchronisable (for \Ptp and \Mailbox) was proposed in \cite{basu_deciding_2016 : the authors claimed that if the set of synchronous send traces is equal to the set of 1-bounded send traces, then the system is synchronisable.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The claim is actually false, as shown in \cite{finkel_synchronizability_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: The authors provide two counterexamples showing that the method is faulty for both \Ptp and \Mailbox.
elements.sent-2411-036: The counterexamples in \cite{finkel_synchronizability_2017 expose cases where the set of 2-bounded send traces contains traces that do not exist in the set of 1-bounded send traces, while the latter are identical to the set of synchronous send traces.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Moreover, checking synchronisability of a system communicating with a \Ptp architecture, is shown to be undecidable in \cite[Theorem 3]{finkel_synchronizability_2017 , while the problem remains open for \Mailbox systems.}
elements.sent-2411-038: In this paper we start answering this last question.
elements.sent-2411-039: In a first attempt to assess the problem, we relax one of the hypothesis in \cite{finkel_synchronizability_2017 and consider the general case of communicating automata with final accepting states.}
elements.sent-2411-040: This allows us to code the Post Correspondence Problem into our formalism and thus prove undecidability of what we call the Generalised Synchronisability Problem.
elements.sent-2411-041: Final states are a key ingredient in the proof which cannot be adapted to the case without accepting states (or said otherwise where all states are accepting).
elements.sent-2411-042: To understand where the expressiveness of the problem lies, we started considering how different topologies of communication --\ie the underlining structure of exchanges-- affects the decidability of the Synchronisability Problem.
elements.sent-2411-043: Our first step generalises another result of \cite{finkel_synchronizability_2017 , where it was shown that Synchronisability Problem is decidable for oriented ring topologies, \cite[Theorem 11]{finkel_synchronizability_2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Here, we consider trees and show that the Synchronisability Problem is decidable.
elements.sent-2411-045: The result is obtained by showing that the language of buffers is regular and can be computed.
elements.sent-2411-046: We believe that our algorithm can be extended to multitrees (which are acyclic graphs where among each pair of nodes there is a single path).
elements.sent-2411-047: Notice that our approach is more direct than the one in \cite{basu_deciding_2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Instead of comparing (regular) sets of send traces we directly analyse the content of buffers.
elements.sent-2411-049: The paper is organised as follows: \cref{sec:prelim introduces the necessary terminology.}
elements.sent-2411-050: The first undecidability result is given in \cref{sec:post , while \cref{sec:decidability discuss the decidability of synchronisability for tree topologies.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: For a finite set $\alphabet$, a word $w= a_1a_2\dots a_n \in \alphabet^*$ is a finite sequence of symbols, such that $a_i \in \alphabet$, for all $1 \leq i \leq n$.
elements.sent-2411-053: The concatenation of two words $\word_1$ and $\word_2$ is denoted $\word_1\concat \word_2$, $\length{\word}$ denotes the length of $\word$, and $\emptyword$ denotes the empty word.
elements.sent-2411-054: We assume some familiarity with non-deterministic finite state automata, and we write $\languageof{\Automaton{ }$ for the language accepted by automaton $\Automaton{}$.}
elements.sent-2411-055: A communicating automaton is a finite state machine that performs actions of two kinds: either sends or receives.
elements.sent-2411-056: A network of communicating automata, or simply network, is the parallel composition of a finite set $\Part$ of participants that exchange messages.
elements.sent-2411-057: We consider a finite set of messages \messageSet.
elements.sent-2411-058: Each message in \messageSet consists of a sender, a receiver, and some finite information.
elements.sent-2411-059: We denote $\msg{a {p {q \in \messageSet$ the message sent from peer $p$ to $q$ with payload $a$, $p \neq q$, \ie a peer can not send/receive messages to/from itself.}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: An action is the send $!m$ or the reception $?m$ of a message $m \in \messageSet$.
elements.sent-2411-061: We denote the set of actions for peer $p$ $\actionSet{p = \{ \send{a {p {q , \rec{a {q {p \mid \msg{a {p {q}\in \messageSet \wedge q \in \Part \}$ and $\sendact{p = \{ \send{a {p {q \mid \msg{a {p {q}\in \messageSet \wedge ~q \in \Part \}$ the set of sends from $p$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: The topology of a system is a graph with arrows from senders to receivers.
elements.sent-2411-063: Its topology is an oriented graph $\topo{\Network = (\vertexSet,\edgeSet) $, where $\vertexSet=\{p \mid p \in \Part\}$ and $\edgeSet = \left\{ (p, q) \mid \exists \msg{a {p {q \in \messageSet \right\}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Let $\senders{p}$ (\resp $\receivers{p}$) be the set of participants sending to (\resp receiving from) $p$.
elements.sent-2411-065: Different semantics can be considered for the same network depending on the communication mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-066: A system is a network together with a communication mechanism, denoted $\System{\Network {com {}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: It can communicate synchronously or asynchronously.
elements.sent-2411-068: In a synchronous system, each message sent is immediately received, \ie the communication exchange cannot be decoupled.
elements.sent-2411-069: In an asynchronous communication instead, messages are stored in a memory.
elements.sent-2411-070: Here we only consider FIFO (First In First Out) buffers, which can be bounded or unbounded.
elements.sent-2411-072: Synchronous (\synch): there is no buffer in the system, messages are immediately received when they are sent;.
elements.sent-2411-073: We use configurations to describe the state of a system and its buffers.
elements.sent-2411-074: A \synch configuration (respectively a \pp configuration, or a \mailbox configuration) is a tuple $\config = \left( (\state{s { {p )_{p \in \Part , \bufferSet\right)$ such that:.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: We write $\emptyword$ to denote an empty buffer, and $\bufferSet^\emptyset$ to denote that all buffers are empty.
elements.sent-2411-076: We write $\bufferSet\{b_i/b\}$ for the tuple of buffers $\bufferSet$, where $\buffer{i}$ is substituted with $\buffer{}$.
elements.sent-2411-077: We denote $\configSet$ the set of all configurations, $\configInit= \left((\state{s {0 {p )_{p\in \Part , \buffersInit \right)$ is the initial configuration, and $\configFinal \subseteq \configSet$ is the set of final configurations, where $\state{s { {p \in \finalStates{p}$ for all participant $p \in \Part$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: We describe the behaviour of a system with runs.
elements.sent-2411-079: A run is a sequence of transitions starting from an initial configuration $\configInit$.
elements.sent-2411-080: Let $\com \in \{\synch,~\pp,~\mailbox\}$ be the type of communication.
elements.sent-2411-081: We define $\TransitionAsync{ { { {\com { ^*$ as the transitive reflexive closure of $\TransitionAsync{ { { {\com {}$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: In order to simplify the definitions of executions and traces (given in what follows) and without loss of generality, we choose to label the transition with the sending message $\send{a {p {q}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: In a synchronous communication, we consider that the send and the receive of a message have done at the same time, \ie the synchronous relation \synch-send merges these two actions.
elements.sent-2411-084: The synchronous system $\System{\Network {\synch {}$ associated with $\Network$ is the smallest binary relation $\xrightarrow[\synch]{}$ over \synch-configurations such that: \begin{prooftree \AxiomC{$\Transition{\state{s { {p {\send{a {p {q {\state{s' { {p {p}$} \AxiomC{$\Transition{\state{s { {q {\rec{a {p {q {\state{s' { {q {q}$} \LeftLabel{(\synch-send) \BinaryInfC{$\TransitionSync{\left( (\state{s { {1 , \dots , \state{s { {p , \dots , \state{s { {q , \dots , \state{s { {n ), \bufferSet^\emptyset\right) {\msg{a {p {q {\left( (\state{s { {1 , \dots , \state{s' { {p , \dots , \state{s' { {q , \dots , \state{s { {n ), \bufferSet^\emptyset\right)}$} \end{prooftree.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: The peer-to-peer system $\System{\Network {\pp {}$ associated with $\Network$ is the least binary relation $\TransitionAsync{ { { {\pp {}$ over $\pp$ configurations such that for each configuration $C = \left( (\state{s { {p )_{p \in \Part , \bufferSet\right)$, we have $\bufferSet = (b_{pq )_{p \neq q \in \Part}$, with $b_{pq}\in~\messageSet^*$, and $\TransitionAsync{ { { {\pp {}$ is the least transition induced by: \begin{prooftree \AxiomC{$\Transition{\state{s { {p {\send{a {p {q {\state{s' { {p {p}$} \LeftLabel{(\pp-send) \UnaryInfC{$\TransitionAsync{\left((\state{s { {1 , \dots , \state{s { {p , \dots , \state{s { {n ), \bufferSet\right) {\send{a {p {q {\left((\state{s { {1 , \dots , \state{s' { {p , \dots , \state{s { {n ), \bufferSet\{b_{pq / b_{pq}\cdot a\}\right) {\pp {}$} \end{prooftree \begin{prooftree \AxiomC{$\Transition{\state{s { {q {\rec{a {p {q {\state{s' { {q {q}$} \AxiomC{$b_{pq = a\cdot b_{pq '$ \LeftLabel{(\pp-rec) \BinaryInfC{ $\TransitionAsync{\left(( \state{s { {1 , \dots , \state{s { {q , \dots , \state{s { {n ), \bufferSet\right) {\rec{a {p {q {\left((\state{s { {1 , \dots , \state{s' { {q , \dots ,\state{s { {n ), \bufferSet\{b_{pq / b_{pq '\}\right) {\pp {}$} \end{prooftree.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: The mailbox system $\System{N {\mailbox {}$ associated with $\Network$ is the smallest binary relation $\TransitionAsync{ { { {\mailbox {}$ over $\mailbox$-configurations such that for each configuration $C = \left( (\state{s { {p )_{p \in \Part , \bufferSet\right)$, we have $\bufferSet= (b_{p )_{p \in \Part}$ and $\TransitionAsync{ { { {\mailbox {}$ is the smallest transition such that: \begin{prooftree \AxiomC{$\Transition{\state{s { {p {\send{a {p {q {\state{s' { {p {p}$} \LeftLabel{(\mailbox-send) \UnaryInfC{ $\TransitionAsync {\left((\state{s { {1 , \dots ,\state{s { {p , \dots ,\state{s { {n ), \bufferSet\right) {\send{a {p {q {\left((\state{s { {1 , \dots ,\state{s' { {p , \dots , \state{s { {n ), \bufferSet\{b_{q / b_{q}\cdot a\}\right) {\mailbox {}$} \end{prooftree \begin{prooftree \AxiomC{$\Transition{\state{s { {q {\rec{a {p {q {\state{s' { {q {q}$}\AxiomC{$b_{q = a\cdot b_{q '$ \LeftLabel{(\mailbox-rec) \BinaryInfC{$\TransitionAsync{\left((\state{s { {1 , \dots ,\state{s { {q , \dots ,\state{s { {n ), \bufferSet\right) {\rec{a {p {q {\left((\state{s { {1 , \dots , \state{s' { {q , \dots , \state{s { {n ), \bufferSet \{b_{q / b_{q '\}\right) {\mailbox {}$} \end{prooftree.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: In order to study the behaviour of systems, we define the set of executions and traces.
elements.sent-2411-088: An execution $\exec$ is a sequence of actions leading to a final global state and the corresponding trace $\trace$ is the projection on the send actions\footnote{In Definition~\ref{def:traces , we decide not to take into consideration the content of buffers, differently to others papers, like \cite{finkel_synchronizability_2017 , where the authors study stable configurations, \ie configurations where buffers are empty.. .}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: If $\word$ is a word over actions, then let $ \word\projOut $ (\resp $ \word\projIn $) be its projection on only send (\resp receive) actions, let $ \word\projPeers{P $ its projection on only actions that involve only the participants in a set $ P $, let $ \word\projPeer{p $ its projection on receives towards $ p $ and sends from $ p $, and let $ \word\projMess $ be the word over messages that results from $ w $ by removing all $ ! $ and $ ? $.}}
elements.sent-2411-090: We extend the operators $ \projOut $, $ \projIn $, $ \projPeers{P $, $ \projPeer{p $, and $ \projMess $ to languages, by applying them on every word of the language.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Note that, $ \word\projPeers{\Set{p $ is always empty, since there are no actions that involve only a single participant $ p $, whereas $ \word\projPeer{p $ is the projection of $ w $ to its actions in that $ p $ has an active role (sender in send actions and receiver in receive actions).}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: A system is synchronisable if its asynchronous behaviour can be related to its synchronous one.
elements.sent-2411-093: Thus, an asynchronous system is synchronisable if its set of traces is the same as the one obtained from the synchronous system.
elements.sent-2411-094: The system $\System{\Network {\com {}$ is synchronisable if and only if $\traceSet{\System{\Network {\com { = \traceSet{\System{\Network {\synch { }$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: We define the Synchronisability Problem as the decision problem of determining whether a given system, where all states are accepting states, is synchronisable or not.
elements.sent-2411-096: We also consider the Generalised Synchronisability Problem without any constraints on the accepting states of the system.
elements.section-2411-003: The Generalised Synchronisability Problem is Undecidable
elements.sent-2411-097: The first contribution is about assessing the undecidability of the Generalised Synchronisability Problem for the \Mailbox semantics.
elements.sent-2411-098: This result strongly relies on the notion of accepting word.
elements.sent-2411-099: Moreover, the entire section considers networks without any constraints on final configurations (\ie $\configFinal \subseteq \configSet$).
elements.sent-2411-100: We will resort to the Post Correspondence Problem (PCP) which is known to be an undecidable decision problem~\cite{post_variant_1946 , to prove that the Generalised Synchronisability Problem is undecidable.}
elements.sent-2411-101: We will show that the encoding of a PCP instance \pcpinstance{ is not synchronisable if and only if the instance has a solution.}
elements.sent-2411-102: An instance \pcpinstance{ of the PCP consists of two finite ordered lists of the same number of non-empty words \begin{equation}\Word= {w_{1}, w_{2},\ldots , w_{n}} and \Word'= {w'_{1}, w'_{2}, \ldots , w'_{n}}\end{equation} such that $w_{i , w'_{i \in \alphabet^{*}$ for all indices $1 \leq i \leq n$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: A solution of this instance is a finite sequence of indices $\Sol = ( i_{1 , i_{2 , \dots, i_{m )$ with $m \geq 1$ and $i_{j \in [1,n]$ for all $1 \leq j \leq m$ such that: \begin{equation}w_{i_{1}} \cdot w_{i_{2}} \cdot \ldots \cdot w_{i_{m}} = w'_{i_{1}} \cdot w'_{i_{2}} \cdot \ldots \cdot w'_{i_{m}} .\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.paragraph-2411-005: Mailbox Encoding of the Post Correspondence Problem.
elements.sent-2411-104: The encoding in \Mailbox systems requires some care.
elements.sent-2411-105: When an automaton is receiving messages from multiple participants, these messages are interleaved in the buffer and it is generally not possible to anticipate in which order these messages have been sent.
elements.sent-2411-106: The encoding of an instance \pcpinstance{ of the PCP is a parallel composition of four automata: $\Automaton{I}$, $\Automaton{W}$, $\Automaton{W'}$, and $\Automaton{L}$, where $\Automaton{I}$ sends the same indices to $\Automaton{W}$ and $\Automaton{W'}$ which in turn send the respective words to $\Automaton{L}$.}
elements.sent-2411-107: The topology and the buffer layout of the system is depicted in Figure~\ref{figure:topologyEncodeMail .}
elements.sent-2411-108: We will explain our encoding over an example.
elements.sent-2411-109: Take the following PCP instance with $\alphabet = \{a, b\}$, $\Word = a, b, abab$ and $\Word' = ba, baa, b$.
elements.sent-2411-110: We know that there is a solution for this instance with $\Sol = (2, 1, 3)$.
elements.sent-2411-111: Figures~\ref{figure:example:encodingMailAutBI --\ref{figure:example:encodingMailAutBL depict the automata solving the PCP instance.}}
elements.sent-2411-112: Automaton $\Automaton{I}$ guesses the sequence of indexes and sends it to both $\Automaton{W}$ and $\Automaton{W'}$.
elements.sent-2411-113: The message $\\begin{equation}is used to signal the end of the sequence. Automaton $\Automaton{W}$ and $\Automaton{W'}$ receive indexes from $\Automaton{I}$ and send the corresponding sequences of letters to $\Automaton{L}$. At the reception of message $\\end{equation}, they send messages $end$ to $\Automaton{L}$.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}is used to signal the end of the sequence. Automaton $\Automaton{W}$ and $\Automaton{W'}$ receive indexes from $\Automaton{I}$ and send the corresponding sequences of letters to $\Automaton{L}$. At the reception of message $\\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-114: Automaton $\Automaton{L}$ checks whether the sequences of letters produced by $\Automaton{W}$ and $\Automaton{W'}$ coincide.
elements.sent-2411-115: Letters from $\Automaton{W}$ and $\Automaton{W'}$ need to be alternate and are read in turn, and the additional receptions are used to make the system synchronisable and to recognize errors (\ie sequences that are not a solution).
elements.sent-2411-116: If all comparisons succeed, included the $end$ messages, then $\Automaton{L}$ sends message $ok$ that is not received by any participant and ends up in the unique accepting state.
elements.sent-2411-117: More formally, we define the encoding as follows.
elements.sent-2411-118: The encoding of $(\Word,\Word')$ is the network $\Encode{\Word,\Word' {\mailbox = \left((\Automaton{p )_{p \in \Part , \messageSet\right)$ where:.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: It is easy to see that in the synchronous semantics the system cannot reach any final configuration, because of message $ok$ which cannot be sent since it cannot be received.
elements.sent-2411-120: The set of traces of the synchronous system is indeed empty.
elements.sent-2411-121: Let \pcpinstance{ an instance of PCP and $\Network = \Encode{\Word,\Word' {\mailbox}$ its encoding into communicating automata.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: In the \Mailbox semantics, message $ok$ can be sent only if the encoded instance of PCP has a solution.
elements.sent-2411-124: If the instance of PCP has no solution, then the \Mailbox system is unable to reach the final configuration and the set of traces is empty.
elements.sent-2411-125: Summing up, the set of traces is not empty if and only if there exists a solution to the corresponding PCP instance.
elements.sent-2411-126: For every instance \pcpinstance{ of PCP, where $\Network = \Encode{\Word,\Word' {\mailbox}$, \pcpinstance{ has a solution if and only if $\traceSet{\System{N {\mailbox { \neq \emptyset$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-127: Let \pcpinstance{ be a PCP instance and $\Network = \Encode{\Word,\Word' {\mailbox}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: Let $\Sol_{\pcpinstance{ = (i_1, i_2, \ldots , i_m)$ be a solution of \pcpinstance{ .}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: Let $w= a_1 \dots a_n$ be the word generated from the sequence of indices.
elements.sent-2411-130: From Definition~\ref{def:encodingMail , it is easy to see that the following execution $t$ is possible and that it leads to a final configuration with the final global state $(\state{q {\$' {I , \state{q {e {W ,\state{q {e {W' , \state{q {ok {L )$: \begin{equation}t = \send{i_1}{I}{W} \cdot \send{i_1}{I}{W'}\cdot \ldots \cdot \send{i_m}{I}{W}\cdot \send{i_m}{I}{W'} \cdot \send{\$}{I}{W}\cdot \send{\$'}{I}{W'} \label{one} \cdot \send{a_1}{W}{L}\cdot \send{a_1'}{W'}{L} \cdot \ldots \cdot \send{a_n}{W}{L}\cdot \send{a_n'}{W'}{L} \cdot \send{end}{W}{L}\cdot \send{end}{W'}{L} \label{two} \cdot \send{ok}{L}{I} \label{three}\end{equation} Part~(\ref{one ) consists of the indices sent by automaton \Automaton{I in turn to the automata \Automaton{W and \Automaton{W' , including the messages $\$, \$'$ that are used to signal the end of the sequence.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: Part~(\ref{two ) contains the letters of word $w$ sent in turn by \Automaton{W and \Automaton{W' upon reception of the corresponding indices to \Automaton{L .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-132: Since we are considering mailbox communication here, note that messages from \Automaton{W and \Automaton{W' must alternate.}}
elements.sent-2411-133: Finally, automaton \Automaton{L having matched all the words from \Automaton{W and \Automaton{W' , including the final $end$ messages is able to send the last message $ok$, part~(\ref{three ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: Since $t \in \traceSet{\System{N {\mailbox { }$, $t$ is the projection on send messages of an accepting execution $t' \in \execSet{\System{\Network {\mailbox { }$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-136: By construction, to reach state $\state{q {ok {L}$ we know that $t' = t_1 \cdot \rec{end {W {L}\cdot \rec{end {W' {L \cdot \send{ok {L {I \send{ok {L {I}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-137: With a similar reasoning, to reach states $\state{q {e {W}$ and $\state{q {e {W'}$, $t_1\projOut = t_2\projOut \cdot \send{end {W {L \cdot \send{end {W' {L}$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-138: This also entails that there has been at least one index sent by automaton \Automaton{I (both to \Automaton{W and \Automaton{W' ).}}}

elements.section-2411-004: Synchronisability of \MAILBOX Communication for Tree-like Topologies
elements.sent-2411-147: We are interested in the Synchronisability Problem, where automata have no final states.
elements.sent-2411-148: Notice that this is equivalent of having automata where all states are final.
elements.sent-2411-149: Thus, we consider networks where all configurations are final configurations ($\configFinal = \configSet$).
elements.sent-2411-150: The encoding in Section \ref{sec:post cannot be used as it strongly relies on the existence of special final configurations that can only be reached in the asynchronous (\Mailbox) semantics.}
elements.sent-2411-151: Moreover, because of the nature of \Mailbox communications, the encoding in \cite{finkel_synchronizability_2017 cannot be used.}
elements.sent-2411-152: In fact, the order of messages received from different recipients becomes important and the relative speeds of the automata ($W$ and $W'$) producing the letters to be compared, cannot be ``synchronised".
elements.sent-2411-153: In order to understand the expressiveness of \Mailbox system, we start by constraining the shape of topologies.. A topology (cfr.
elements.sent-2411-154: Definition \ref{def:topology ) is the underlining communication structure marking the direction of communication among participants.}
elements.sent-2411-155: Here we start by considering topologies that form a tree and we want to understand whether the topology impacts (or not) the decidability of the Synchronisability Problem.
elements.sent-2411-156: When considering tree topologies, each of the inner automata (nodes) receives messages by only one other participant.
elements.sent-2411-157: Because of this, systems with tree topologies will have the same set of executions for both \Mailbox and \Ptp semantics.
elements.sent-2411-158: Let $ \rootP \in \Part $ denote the root of the tree, \ie $ \senders{\rootP $ is empty.}
elements.sent-2411-159: Notice that $ \senders{p $ is a singleton for all inner nodes $ p\in \Part\setminus\Set{\rootP $.}}
elements.sent-2411-160: It is interesting to see that we can characterise an algorithm to check whether a system is synchronisable or not.
elements.sent-2411-161: To this aim, a system needs to validate two conditions:.
elements.sent-2411-162: The main idea is to use the tree structure to capture the influence the automata have on the language of each other.
elements.sent-2411-163: The receptions of an automaton depend only on the availability of matching incoming messages, \ie in a tree by the sends of at most one parent.
elements.sent-2411-164: We compute the influenced language of $ \Automaton{p $, denoted $ \LangTree{\Automaton{p $, considering only the influence of its parent but not of its children.}}}
elements.sent-2411-165: These languages have to be computed from the root $ \rootP $---that does not depend on anybody---towards the leafs of the tree.
elements.sent-2411-166: For an inner node of the tree the possible sequences of outputs of the respective (unique) parent node determine the possible sequences of inputs it can perform and thus the outputs that can be unguarded.
elements.sent-2411-167: The languages $ \LangTreeIn{\Automaton{p $ and $ \LangTreeOut{\Automaton{p $ are its respective projections on only receives or sends.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-168: We define the influenced language as follows: \begin{equation}\LangTree{\Automaton{p}} =  \languageof{\Automaton{\rootP}} if  p = \rootP \Set{w \mid w \in \languageof{\Automaton{p}} \wedge \left( w\projIn \right)\projMess \in \left( \LangTreeOut{\Automaton{q}} \right)\projMess \wedge \senders{p} = \Set{q} } otherwise  \LangTreeIn{\Automaton{p}} = \LangTree{\Automaton{p}}\projIn \LangTreeOut{\Automaton{p}} = \LangTree{\Automaton{p}}\projOut\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-169: Since the root does not receive any message, it is not influenced by any parent.
elements.sent-2411-171: For any inner node $ p \in \Part $ of the tree, we allow only words with input sequences that match a sequence of outputs of its parent $ q $ influenced language.
elements.sent-2411-172: To match inputs with their corresponding outputs, we ignore the signs $ ! $ and $ ? $ using the projection $ \projMess $.
elements.sent-2411-173: Then $ \LangTree{\Automaton{p $ contains the words of $ \Automaton{p $ that respect the possible input sequences induced by the parent $ q $.}}}

elements.sent-2411-176: In synchronous communication, sends and receptions are blocking, \ie they have to wait for matching communication partners.
elements.sent-2411-177: In asynchronous communication with unbounded buffers, only inputs are blocking, whereas all outputs can be performed immediately.
elements.sent-2411-178: Hence, for synchronisability the automata should provide matching inputs whenever their communication partners are ready to send.
elements.sent-2411-179: We use causality to check for this condition.
elements.sent-2411-180: For some automaton $ \Automaton{p $, action $ a_2 $ causally depends on action $ a_1 $, denoted as $ a_1 <_{p a_2 $, if for all $ w \in \LangTree{\Automaton{p $ action $ a_2 $ does not occur or $ a_1 $ occurs before $ a_2 $.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-181: First, we have to check that in no automata we find a relation of the form $ !x <_p ?y $, because such a dependency always leads to non-synchronisability.

elements.sent-2411-276: In this paper, we start answering a problem that have remained open since \cite{finkel_synchronizability_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-277: We first have shown that the Generalised Synchronisability Problem is undecidable for \Mailbox systems.
elements.sent-2411-278: The undecidability result cannot be easily adpated to communicating automata without final states as the role of automaton \Automaton{L (\ie the comparator) is made more complex by the fact that letters that end up in its buffer are mixed between those coming from \Automaton{W and \Automaton{W' .}}}
elements.sent-2411-279: This would require an additional synchronisation between \Automaton{W and \Automaton{W' which would mess the exchanges between those automata and \Automaton{I .}}}
elements.sent-2411-280: Hence, in an attempt to get closer to a proof of decidability for Synchronisability Problem, we considered tree topologies.
elements.sent-2411-281: We have presented an algorithm to decide synchronisability for systems that feature a tree topology.
elements.sent-2411-282: The key ingredient in the above algorithm for trees is, that we can compute the languages $ \LangTree{\Automaton{p $ and thus the possible behaviour for every node, by starting from the root and following the unique path from the root to the respective node.}}

elements.sent-2411-285: By observing that a forest is synchronisable if and only if each of its trees is synchronisable.
elements.sent-2411-286: Moreover reversed trees and multitrees have the same property of featuring unique paths between any two nodes.
